docs.text: HYROX race analytics pyrox-client A Python client for HYROX race results that keeps analysis fast, reliable, and reproducible. Pull full races as pandas DataFrames, apply rigorous filters, and build high-signal performance models. Get started Analytics Clean race pulls Download race data straight from the CDN and cache it locally for repeatable work. Clear filters Server-side gender and division filters plus exact time-window slicing in minutes. Analysis ready Station and run splits are already renamed and normalized for modeling. Quickstart ¶ import pyrox client = pyrox . PyroxClient () races = client . list_races ( season = 7 ) print ( races . head ()) london = client . get_race ( season = 7 , location = "london" , gender = "male" ) print ( london [ "total_time" ] . describe ()) Use this documentation as a map: start with Quickstart, then move into Filtering, Data Model, and the Analytics page once you are ready to dive into your data/performance.

docs.text: Project State ¶ Not tracked in git. Working notes on current state of the analytics workflow. Hardening: rate limiting + error sanitization (2026-06-13) ¶ pyrox_api_service/ratelimit.py (new): per-client-IP edge limiter behind a pure-ASGI RateLimitMiddleware , keyed off Fly-Client-IP . Added once , on the composed app ( app.py ), which wraps both the REST routes and the mounted /mcp sub-app — adding it to the sub-app too double-counts MCP requests. The /mcp 307→ /mcp/ redirect is handled by charging a slot only on the served (non-redirect) response ( test() to gate, hit() on a non-3xx response), so each logical call counts once. Header-less requests (in-process MCP→REST calls, tests) are exempt. Default 60/minute , override via PYROX_RATE_LIMIT . Uses the limits library (added to the api extra) directly — slowapi 's SlowAPIMiddleware was rejected: it's route-endpoint coupled (unreliable on the mounted MCP sub-app) and raises AttributeError on the request-exempt path. Tested in tests/test_ratelimit.py (incl. a mount+redirect single-count guard). app.py _raise_http : the DatabaseConfigurationError → 500 branch now logs the real cause and returns a generic "internal server error" instead of leaking the DuckDB path. 400/404 messages (mistake-recovery) unchanged. Still deferred (decided 2026-06-13) ¶ /mcp auth — endpoint stays open for now (bearer-token plan shelved). Fly scale-to-zero / VM right-sizing — machine stays always-on for now. DNS-rebind protection is wired ( PYROX_MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS ) but the env var is not set in production yet. MCP server (built 2026-06-13) ¶ Goal: let Claude (and later Codex) answer natural-language questions against the HYROX dataset by connecting to a remote MCP server. Design captured in CONTEXT.md and docs/adr/0001-mcp-over-http-reporting-service.md . What exists ¶ GET /api/distribution ( pyrox_api_service/app.py ): the only new backend endpoint. Returns histogram bins + summary stats for a cohort. gender required; season defaults to the latest season for the cohort; division defaults to open ; metric defaults to overall . Cohorts with n < 30 ( DISTRIBUTION_SMALL_SAMPLE_MIN_N ) come back with small_sample: true , suppressed tail percentiles ( p10 / p90 → null), and a note . Tested in tests/test_api.py (8 cases). pyrox_api_service/mcp_tools.py : seven intent-shaped tool functions ( list_filters , find_athlete , get_distribution , get_rankings , get_race_report , get_deepdive , get_athlete_profile ). They call the REST app in-process via a Starlette TestClient (no network socket) and return error dicts instead of raising. List tools are compact (capped + total). Tested in tests/test_mcp_tools.py . pyrox_api_service/mcp_app.py : registers the tools with FastMCP ( stateless_http=True ) and mounts the streamable-HTTP app at /mcp . Composed app served via pyrox_api_service.mcp_app:app (Dockerfile updated). Dependency mcp>=1.2.0 added to the api extra in pyproject.toml . Verified ¶ Full suite: 94 passed, 3 skipped (integration). Ruff clean. End-to-end MCP smoke: initialize → tools/list (7 tools) → tools/call list_filters returns real cohort data, with the REST API still live on the same app. Not done / deferred ¶ Pagination for list tools is approximated by an adjustable limit (the /api/rankings endpoint has no offset). Add real offset only if needed. No auth (v1 decision). PYROX_MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS / _ORIGINS enable DNS-rebinding protection if an operator wants to lock to the Fly host. Rate-limiting now implemented at both edges (see the hardening section above). small_sample threshold (30) and default division ( open ) were chosen from reasoning, not from the data — worth tuning against actual cohort-size distributions in pyrox_duckdb . Claude-side plotting from returned bins is by design (no server-rendered images); not separately tested here.

docs.text: Analytics ¶ A focused set of workflows to extract signal from race data. 1. Segment z-scores ¶ Normalize each station split relative to the field to see strengths and weaknesses. import numpy as np import pandas as pd race = client . get_race ( season = 7 , location = "london" , gender = "male" ) split_cols = [ "skiErg_time" , "sledPush_time" , "sledPull_time" , "burpeeBroadJump_time" , "rowErg_time" , "farmersCarry_time" , "sandbagLunges_time" , "wallBalls_time" , ] field = race [ split_cols ] . astype ( float ) means = field . mean () stds = field . std ( ddof = 0 ) . replace ( 0 , np . nan ) z = ( field - means ) / stds race_z = pd . concat ([ race [[ "name" ]], z ], axis = 1 ) 2. Rank percentiles by total time ¶ race = client.get_race(season=7, location="london") race = race.sort_values("total_time") race["percentile"] = race["total_time"].rank(pct=True) 3. Create pace buckets ¶ bins = [0, 55, 60, 65, 70, 999] labels = ["<55", "55-60", "60-65", "65-70", "70+"] race["pace_bucket"] = pd.cut(race["total_time"], bins=bins, labels=labels) summary = race.groupby("pace_bucket")["total_time"].agg(["count", "mean"]) 4. Build a station share profile ¶ Identify which stations dominate a given athlete's time. athlete = client . get_athlete_in_race ( season = 7 , location = "london" , athlete_name = "surname, name" , ) split_cols = [ "skiErg_time", "sledPush_time", "sledPull_time", "burpeeBroadJump_time", "rowErg_time", "farmersCarry_time", "sandbagLunges_time", "wallBalls_time", ] row = athlete . iloc [ 0 ] share = row [ split_cols ] / row [ split_cols ] . sum ()

docs.text: Client API ¶ PyroxClient ¶ Create a client: from pyrox import PyroxClient client = PyroxClient () list_races(season: int | None = None, force_refresh: bool = False) ¶ Return a DataFrame of available races. Filter by season when provided. list_seasons(force_refresh: bool = False) ¶ Return sorted season values available in the manifest. list_locations(season: int | None = None, force_refresh: bool = False) ¶ Return sorted location values available in the manifest. When season is provided, results are limited to that season. Returns an empty list if no locations match. list_years(...) ¶ list_years ( season : int | None = None , location : str | None = None , force_refresh : bool = False , ) -> list [ int ] Return sorted calendar years available in the manifest. Location matching is case-insensitive. Returns an empty list if no years match. get_race(...) ¶ get_race ( season : int , location : str , year : int | None = None , gender : str | None = None , division : str | None = None , total_time : float | tuple [ float | None , float | None ] | None = None , use_cache : bool = True , ) -> pd . DataFrame Key behaviors: - Applies server-side gender and division filters when available. - Converts time columns into minutes. - Supports strict time windows using total_time . Division values seen in the dataset include open , pro , and pro_doubles . If the manifest lookup fails, RaceNotFound includes discovery context such as available seasons, available years, and close location suggestions: from pyrox.errors import RaceNotFound try : client . get_race ( season = 8 , location = "londn" ) except RaceNotFound as exc : print ( exc . suggestions ) get_athlete_in_race(...) ¶ get_athlete_in_race ( season : int , location : str , athlete_name : str , year : int | None = None , gender : str | None = None , division : str | None = None , use_cache : bool = True , ) -> pd . DataFrame Case-insensitive search on the name column. Raises AthleteNotFound if no match. get_season(...) ¶ get_season ( season : int , locations : Iterable [ str ] | None = None , gender : str | None = None , division : str | None = None , max_workers : int = 8 , use_cache : bool = True , ) -> pd . DataFrame Parallelized race fetching with a configurable worker pool. clear_cache(pattern: str = "*") ¶ Clear cached items matching a glob pattern. cache_info() -> dict ¶ Return cache statistics and keys. Reporting Service Note (Repository-Only) ¶ This repository also ships a FastAPI reporting service ( pyrox_api_service/ ) that is separate from the published pyrox-client wheel. The optional pyrox-client[reporting] extra installs the DuckDB Python library needed by ReportingClient , but it does not bundle a DuckDB database file. Use ReportingClient(database="/path/to/pyrox_duckdb") when you have a local generated database artifact. For athlete profile endpoints: GET /api/athletes/profile?name=<name> GET /api/athletes/{athlete_id}/profile personal_bests entries may include an optional percentile field (float in [0, 1] ) per segment key ( overall , runplusroxzone , skierg , sledpush , sledpull , burpeebroadjump , rowerg , farmerscarry , sandbaglunges , wallballs ). average_times entries may also include optional percentile with the same range and segment keys. Percentile direction matches report split percentiles: - 1.0 means faster than 100% of the cohort. - 0.0 means slower than everyone in the cohort. - If percentile cannot be computed for a segment, the key is omitted for that segment without failing the endpoint. Profile percentiles are computed against historical results in the same division and gender.

docs.text: Caching ¶ Pyrox caches results locally for speed and repeatability. Cache entries store the DataFrame plus metadata and ETags when available. Defaults ¶ Cache dir: ~/.cache/pyrox Manifest TTL: 2 hours Race TTL: 2 hours Season TTL: 1 hour Opt out per call ¶ race = client.get_race(season=7, location="london", use_cache=False) Clear cache ¶ client.clear_cache() client.clear_cache(pattern="race_7_london*") Inspect cache ¶ info = client.cache_info() print(info) Fields include cache_dir , total_items , total_size_mb , and cached keys.

docs.text: Data model ¶ Each race is returned as a pandas DataFrame where each row represents a single entry (athlete or doubles pair, depending on the race). Common columns ¶ Expect these columns in most races: name : athlete name as shown on the official results site. gender : male | female | mixed . division : open | pro | pro_doubles (case preserved as stored). total_time : total race time, minutes (float). work_time : total station time, minutes. roxzone_time : transition time, minutes. run_time : total running time, minutes. Station and run splits are normalized into readable names: skiErg_time sledPush_time sledPull_time burpeeBroadJump_time rowErg_time farmersCarry_time sandbagLunges_time wallBalls_time run1_time run2_time run3_time run4_time run5_time run6_time run7_time run8_time Time normalization ¶ All time columns are converted into minutes on load. Use them directly in statistical workflows without re-parsing. Schema drift ¶ The upstream data can evolve between seasons. Always check: print(df.columns) If a column is missing, adapt your pipeline rather than assuming it is always present.

docs.text: DuckDB Stabilization Tasks ¶ Define schema contracts for race_results , athletes , athlete_results , and athlete_index Add a metadata table with schema_version, build timestamp, and data source info Store the hash/version of scripts/sql_queries.py and ingest macro in metadata Validate ingest output (tables exist, required columns, non-zero row counts) Add a client-side health check for schema_version mismatch Build a minimal fixture DuckDB for tests (singles + doubles cases) Document required env vars and lock behavior (read-only vs read-write) Remote DuckDB Usage Options ¶ Publish the .duckdb file to object storage and download/cache locally Query Parquet directly from S3 (no DB file; requires on-the-fly views) Use a hosted DuckDB service (e.g., MotherDuck) for multi-user access Use a database server (Postgres/ClickHouse) if concurrent writes are needed Schema Contracts (v1) ¶ Source of truth: scripts/sql_queries.py and scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py . race_results ¶ Built from S3 parquet via CREATE_RACE_RESULTS . Non-nullable fields are required for stable keys and search; other fields may be NULL depending on source data. Keys and required fields: result_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (deterministic md5, primary key) season INTEGER NOT NULL location VARCHAR NOT NULL (lowercased) year INTEGER NOT NULL event_id VARCHAR NOT NULL name VARCHAR NOT NULL (trimmed) Nullable attributes: age_group VARCHAR division VARCHAR event_name VARCHAR gender VARCHAR name_raw VARCHAR nationality VARCHAR Time string columns (VARCHAR, nullable): roxzone_time run1_time , run2_time , run3_time , run4_time , run5_time , run6_time , run7_time , run8_time run_time , total_time skiErg_time , sledPush_time , sledPull_time , burpeeBroadJump_time , rowErg_time , farmersCarry_time , sandbagLunges_time , wallBalls_time work_time Time numeric columns (DOUBLE, nullable; minutes): roxzone_time_min run1_time_min , run2_time_min , run3_time_min , run4_time_min , run5_time_min , run6_time_min , run7_time_min , run8_time_min run_time_min , total_time_min skiErg_time_min , sledPush_time_min , sledPull_time_min , burpeeBroadJump_time_min , rowErg_time_min , farmersCarry_time_min , sandbagLunges_time_min , wallBalls_time_min work_time_min athletes ¶ Derived from race_results (canonical identity). Columns: athlete_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (md5 of canonical_name|gender|nationality, primary key) canonical_name VARCHAR NOT NULL (lower(trim(name_raw))) gender VARCHAR nationality VARCHAR athlete_results ¶ Link table between athletes and race results. Columns: athlete_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (FK to athletes.athlete_id) result_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (FK to race_results.result_id) link_confidence DOUBLE NOT NULL (currently 1.0 for exact match) link_method VARCHAR NOT NULL (currently "exact_key") Constraints: Composite primary key ( athlete_id , result_id ) athlete_index ¶ Pre-aggregated search index for fast lookup. Columns: athlete_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (FK to athletes.athlete_id) canonical_name VARCHAR NOT NULL name_lc VARCHAR NOT NULL (lower(canonical_name)) gender VARCHAR nationality VARCHAR race_count INTEGER NOT NULL avg_total_time DOUBLE avg_run_ratio DOUBLE Constraints: Primary key ( athlete_id ) Contract Alignment Notes ¶ Tests should mirror contract columns (especially athlete_results link metadata). race_results.division is required for client-side filtering in src/pyrox/reporting.py . Build Integrity Gate ¶ The DuckDB ingest refuses to publish a build when any (season, location, year, division) group in race_results shows duplicate-roster fan-out: high rows per distinct athlete name within the same Race and Division. Explicit zero Roxzone values are allowed downstream and do not block the build. The gate is intentionally read-only; contaminated source partitions must be re-scraped or cleaned upstream. Set ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 only for an explicit temporary backfill-window override.

Keys and required fields: result_id VARCHAR NOT NULL (deterministic md5, primary key) season INTEGER NOT NULL location VARCHAR NOT NULL (lowercased) year INTEGER NOT NULL event_id VARCHAR NOT NULL name VARCHAR NOT NULL (trimmed) Nullable attributes: age_group VARCHAR division VARCHAR event_name VARCHAR gender VARCHAR name_raw VARCHAR nationality VARCHAR Time string columns (VARCHAR, nullable): roxzone_time run1_time , run2_time , run3_time , run4_time , run5_time , run6_time , run7_time , run8_time run_time , total_time skiErg_time , sledPush_time , sledPull_time , burpeeBroadJump_time , rowErg_time , farmersCarry_time , sandbagLunges_time , wallBalls_time work_time Time numeric columns (DOUBLE, nullable; minutes): roxzone_time_min run1_time_min , run2_time_min , run3_time_min , run4_time_min , run5_time_min , run6_time_min , run7_time_min , run8_time_min run_time_min , total_time_min skiErg_time_min , sledPush_time_min , sledPull_time_min , burpeeBroadJump_time_min , rowErg_time_min , farmersCarry_time_min , sandbagLunges_time_min , wallBalls_time_min work_time_min

docs.text: The DuckDB ingest refuses to publish a build when any (season, location, year, division) group in race_results shows duplicate-roster fan-out: high rows per distinct athlete name within the same Race and Division. Explicit zero Roxzone values are allowed downstream and do not block the build. The gate is intentionally read-only; contaminated source partitions must be re-scraped or cleaned upstream. Set ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 only for an explicit temporary backfill-window override.
docs.title: Build Integrity Gate

docs.text: Errors ¶ Pyrox exposes a small, predictable hierarchy so your pipelines can fail fast and recover gracefully. PyroxError ¶ Base class for client-specific errors. Catch this for broad error handling. RaceNotFound ¶ Raised when a season/location pair is missing from the manifest or a filter produces zero rows. AthleteNotFound ¶ Raised when the athlete name filter returns no matches. FileNotFoundError ¶ Raised when CDN reads fail unexpectedly. Consider retrying or logging the failing race metadata. Reporting Service Degradation Behavior ¶ For repository-only FastAPI profile endpoints ( /api/athletes/profile and /api/athletes/{athlete_id}/profile ), segment percentile enrichment is best-effort: If cohort data or required columns are missing for a segment, the endpoint omits personal_bests[segment].percentile and/or average_times[segment].percentile . The profile response still returns 200 when the underlying athlete profile exists.

docs.text: Filtering ¶ Pyrox supports high-signal filtering at read time, so you can load only the rows you need. Gender and division ¶ These filters are applied server-side for efficiency. race = client.get_race( season=7, location="london", gender="male", # "male" | "female" | "mixed" division="open", # "open" | "pro" | "pro_doubles" ) Notes: - Values are case-sensitive in the underlying parquet filter; prefer lowercase. - If the filtered dataset has no rows, Pyrox raises RaceNotFound . Total time windows ¶ total_time is expressed in minutes. You can pass a single value or an open interval. # Under 60 minutes sub_60 = client.get_race(season=7, location="london", total_time=60) # Open interval: 50 < total_time < 60 mid_pack = client.get_race(season=7, location="london", total_time=(50, 60)) # Only lower bound slow = client.get_race(season=7, location="london", total_time=(70, None)) Notes: - Bounds are strict ( > and < ), not inclusive. - Filtering happens after time columns are converted to minutes.

docs.text: Try Pyrox MCP ¶ Pyrox exposes a public, read-only MCP server at: https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ Use it when you want Claude to answer natural-language questions about HYROX Race Results without downloading the DuckDB artifact or writing Python. The connector uses curated server-computed analytics: it does not expose raw SQL, arbitrary row exports, or write operations. Add It To Claude Code ¶ Register the connector: claude mcp add --transport http pyrox https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ Verify it is available: claude mcp list By default this registers the connector for the current project. To make it available across your Claude Code projects: claude mcp add --transport http --scope user pyrox https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ Remove it later with: claude mcp remove pyrox What It Can Answer ¶ The MCP server exposes ten intent-shaped tools: Tool Use it for list_filters Discover available seasons, divisions, genders, locations, and age groups. list_races Find valid season and location pairs before asking for race-level stats. find_athlete Resolve an athlete name to candidate Results with result_id values. get_race_report Inspect one Result's full Race report and Segment breakdown. get_athlete_profile Summarize an athlete's historical Results and personal bests. get_distribution Get a Cohort Distribution as histogram bins and summary stats. get_race_summary Get timing stats for all Segments in one Race. get_cohort_segment_averages Compare Run and Station averages for all, top-N, or bottom-N athletes in one Race. get_rankings Query leaderboard rows and hypothetical target-time placement. get_deepdive Compare one Result against cross-location Cohorts for a metric. Example Prompts ¶ Start by discovering the available filters: Using Pyrox, what seasons, divisions, genders, and locations are available? Find an athlete and inspect one Result: Using Pyrox, find results for James Ingham. Pick the most recent open Result and summarize the Race report, including strongest and weakest Segments. Compare a target finish time: Using Pyrox, where would a 62-minute male open time rank in season 8? Ask for a Cohort Distribution: Using Pyrox, show the Distribution of female open finish times in season 8. State the Cohort, sample size, and whether the sample is thin. Build a profile: Using Pyrox, create an athlete profile for James Ingham and explain how his Segment strengths compare with his historical Results. How To Read The Answers ¶ Pyrox uses these terms consistently: Race : one HYROX event instance, identified by season, location, and year. Result : one athlete's performance in one Race. Segment : one timed Run or Station. Roxzone : transition time between Segments. Cohort : the reference population used for rankings, Distributions, and Percentiles. Distribution : histogram bins plus summary stats for a metric across a Cohort. When a user names an athlete, Claude usually needs to follow this flow: find_athlete -> choose a result_id -> get_race_report or get_deepdive The result_id is an internal identifier. Users do not need to know it in advance. For common or ambiguous names, find_athlete returns candidate races so Claude can choose the right Result or ask for a narrower filter. Caveats ¶ Pyrox is based on public HYROX source data and may inherit source-data issues. Thin Cohorts are flagged with sample-size information and should be interpreted cautiously. Divisions are not pooled by default because open, pro, doubles, and relay Results are not directly comparable. Distributions default to the latest season and the open division when those filters are omitted. Claude renders any charts from returned structured data; the server does not return image files. The public MCP endpoint is open and read-only. It is rate-limited to protect availability. Maintainer Smoke Test ¶ Maintainers can verify the live connector with: PYROX_MCP_URL = https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ uv run python scripts/smoke_mcp.py --json
docs.title: Try Pyrox MCP

docs.text: Privacy Policy ¶ Effective date: February 11, 2026 This Privacy Policy explains how Pyrox handles information when you use the Pyrox iOS app, web UI, and related API. Who operates Pyrox ¶ Pyrox is an independent project. The app and API are currently served from: App/web client: Pyrox UI API endpoint: https://pyrox-api.fly.dev Project repository: https://github.com/vmatei2/pyrox-client Information we process ¶ Pyrox is designed to work without account creation. We may process: Query inputs you provide in the app (for example athlete name searches, race filters, and report parameters). Technical request data needed to operate the service (for example IP address, user agent, request timestamps, and error logs). We do not intentionally collect sensitive personal data, payment details, or precise location data. How we use information ¶ We use information to: Return requested analytics and reports. Maintain service reliability, security, and performance. Investigate and fix bugs or abuse. Sharing ¶ We do not sell personal data. Data may be processed by infrastructure providers that host or operate the service (for example cloud hosting and networking providers) strictly to run Pyrox. Data retention ¶ Operational logs and request metadata are retained only as long as reasonably needed for reliability, security, and troubleshooting, then deleted or rotated. Your choices ¶ You can stop using the app at any time. If you have a privacy request, contact us using the project support channel below. Children's privacy ¶ Pyrox is not directed to children under 13, and we do not knowingly collect personal information from children under 13. Changes to this policy ¶ We may update this policy from time to time. Material updates will be posted on this page with a new effective date. Contact ¶ For privacy or support requests, use: https://github.com/vmatei2/pyrox-client/issues

docs.text: Reproducible research ¶ This guide documents the workflow for the notebook example_notebooks/impact_of_race_locations.ipynb . The goal is to make results repeatable, auditable, and easy to refresh. What the notebook does ¶ Pulls Season 7 Open Singles data. Cleans and filters timing fields. Compares event distributions for total/work time by gender. Fits a regression with event-level interaction effects. Produces plots to visualize event differences. Dependencies ¶ The notebook relies on: pyrox-client pandas , numpy matplotlib , seaborn statsmodels If you are using uv (recommended): uv pip install -e . If you do not already have Jupyter installed: uv pip install jupyter Run the notebook ¶ From the repo root: uv run jupyter notebook example_notebooks/impact_of_race_locations.ipynb Alternative: uv run jupyter lab Then open the notebook and run cells top to bottom. Data determinism ¶ The notebook pulls from the live CDN. To keep results stable across runs: Avoid force_refresh=True unless you intend to refresh the dataset. Keep cached data between runs. Default cache location is ~/.cache/pyrox . If you need a clean refresh, clear the cache before running: python - << 'PY' import pyrox client = pyrox . PyroxClient () client . clear_cache () PY Inputs and outputs ¶ Inputs: - Season 7 race data ( get_season(season=7, division="open") ). - Event list in events_to_analyse . Outputs: - Plots in the event_dists/ directory (created automatically). - Regression summary in the notebook output. Repro tips ¶ Keep a copy of the events_to_analyse list in the notebook output so the exact event set is recorded. If you publish results, consider exporting the dataset to a dated parquet file to preserve the snapshot used for modeling.

docs.text: Expose Pyrox to LLMs via an MCP server over the HTTP reporting service ¶ Context ¶ We want Claude (and later Codex) to answer natural-language questions against the HYROX dataset ("what's the distribution of men's results?"). The ReportingClient already offers a raw query(sql) escape hatch and plot_cohort_distribution() against the local 817MB DuckDB file, which is the most flexible path. Decision ¶ The MCP server wraps the hosted FastAPI reporting service over HTTP , exposing a small set of intent-shaped tools rather than the raw ReportingClient . The MCP endpoint is mounted on the existing pyrox_api_service ASGI app and deployed to the same Fly app; users connect by adding one URL as a remote connector. Aggregates (distributions, percentiles) are computed server-side — including a new thin /api/distribution endpoint — and tools return structured data only , leaving chart rendering to the model's own code tool. Considered Options ¶ Wrap ReportingClient / expose query(sql) directly. Rejected: raw SQL over a public connector is an injection / runaway-query / cost surface, requires hosting the 817MB DB with the MCP layer, and pushes cohort-definition and analysis correctness into ad-hoc model-generated code (non-deterministic, easy to get subtly wrong). Let the model compute aggregates from raw rows. Rejected: re-introduces the raw-data-dump cost and makes cohort/normalization logic non-reproducible. Consequences ¶ The cohort definition lives in exactly one place (server), so distributions and percentiles are deterministic and reproducible across runs and clients. New question types that the curated endpoints don't cover require a backend change (a new endpoint), not just a prompt tweak — a deliberate trade of flexibility for correctness and safety. Clients without a code/analysis tool receive data but no rendered chart.
docs.title: Expose Pyrox to LLMs via an MCP server over the HTTP reporting service

docs.text: Bug report: phantom duplicate races (cross-division & cross-event) ¶ Status: Open — root cause located in the data pipeline; fix not yet implemented. Severity: High. Corrupts ~1.5% of race_results rows (~14.7k phantom rows) and silently inflates athlete profiles, rankings, distributions, and any cohort built from the affected events. Discovered via: find_athlete("James Ingham") returning impossible races. Area: Upstream scraper (separate repo, see §0) → source parquet in S3 → warehouse build in this repo. Not an API/MCP logic bug — the service faithfully serves corrupt source rows. Date filed: 2026-06-16. 0. Exact file locations (two separate repos) ¶ The data flows through two distinct repositories on disk . The bug originates in the first; the second only reflects it. A. Upstream scraper repo — produces the source parquet in S3 (this is where the root cause lives): - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py — core scraper: discovers events, parses results per division/gender, writes one parquet per event to S3. - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/scraping_helpers.py - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/generate_manifest.py - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/dispatch_downstream.py - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/pipeline_reporting.py - /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_loader.py B. This repo ( pyrox-client ) — reads that S3 parquet and builds the DuckDB artifact served by the API/MCP: - /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py — orchestrates the DuckDB build. - /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/sql_queries.py — the SQL transforms ( create_race_results_query , rankings, split percentiles). This faithfully copies whatever the parquet contains — no dedup. - /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/pyrox_duckdb — the built artifact (~1.1 GB). - /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/pyrox_api_service/reporting_queries.py + /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/src/pyrox/reporting.py — API/MCP read paths (correct; not the bug). ⚠️ Do not confuse the feeder with /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/scrape_hyresult_for_dates.py . That script only scrapes event start dates (it produces scripts/EVENT_START_DATES_SEASON_*.json ); it does not scrape the result rosters and is unrelated to this bug. The roster scraper is repo A above. 1. Symptom (what the user saw) ¶ James Ingham (singles, GBR, athlete_id = 7ffd7bcf1bba0ad3e184ca89249f21b7 ) has 21 race rows , but several are clearly impossible: he appears to have run the exact same time (1:07:18) to the second at four different venues on four different dates , across every division (open / pro / doubles / pro_doubles) — at events he never physically competed in. His 21 rows split into 9 genuine races and 12 phantom duplicate rows : status location division total_time start_date PHANTOM apac-championship-brisbane open / pro / doubles / pro_doubles 1:07:18 2026-04-12 PHANTOM warsaw open / pro / doubles / pro_doubles 1:07:18 2026-04-19 PHANTOM cardiff pro / pro_doubles 1:07:18 2026-04-29 PHANTOM lisboa pro / pro_doubles 1:07:18 2026-05-01 genuine berlin open 1:02:34 2026-05-22 genuine singapore-expo open 1:02:56 2025-11-29 genuine hamburg open 1:04:38 2025-10-02 genuine valencia open 1:04:54 2025-10-17 genuine cape-town open 1:05:19 2025-07-19 genuine shenzhen pro 1:07:33 2025-12-20 genuine bangkok pro 1:09:58 2026-03-20 genuine manchester open 1:10:50 2024-11-09 genuine birmingham open 1:12:45 2024-10-25 Note on bangkok (flagged as suspect in the original report): James's bangkok row (1:09:58) is unique — its split signature does not appear anywhere else — so it is not a phantom duplicate. Likewise his cape-town row is genuine even though cape-town belongs to a contaminated cluster (see §5). Venue membership in a corrupt event does not by itself make a row phantom. The reliable test is whether the exact split signature is replicated elsewhere (see §6). All 12 phantom rows are byte-identical in every split , not just total time: run1 0:04:30 | skiErg 0:04:18 | sledPush 0:03:34 | ... | wallBalls 0:05:13 | run8 0:04:40 | roxzone 0:00:00 | work 0:31:30 ( roxzone 0:00:00 is itself implausible for a real singles race and is a secondary tell that these events are corrupt.) 2. How to reproduce ¶ Via the MCP tool (what a future agent will hit) ¶ find_athlete(name="James Ingham") Returns an ambiguous-match error listing concatenated doubles names — ignore that , it is a separate, expected behaviour (names are not the bug). Resolve the singles athlete directly: # athlete_id for singles James Ingham get_athlete_profile ( athlete_id = "7ffd7bcf1bba0ad3e184ca89249f21b7" ) The profile's race list contains the 12 phantom 1:07:18 rows. Via SQL (direct, read-only) ¶ import duckdb con = duckdb . connect ( "/Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/pyrox_duckdb" , read_only = True ) # 1.1 GB artifact con . execute ( """ SELECT r.location, r.division, r.total_time, r.start_date FROM race_results r JOIN athlete_results ar ON ar.result_id = r.result_id WHERE ar.athlete_id = '7ffd7bcf1bba0ad3e184ca89249f21b7' ORDER BY r.total_time_min, r.location """ ) . fetchdf () 3. The two (three) failure modes ¶ The corruption is not a single bug; the same underlying mishandling shows up as distinct shapes. Mode A — cross-division fan-out (largest) ¶ Within one event, a single result is replicated once per division present in that event , each copy stamped with a different (mostly wrong) division but otherwise identical (same splits, same times). Evidence — warsaw event LR3MS4JI1532 : division rows distinct names doubles 653 653 open 653 653 pro 653 653 pro_doubles 653 653 All four divisions carry the same 653-athlete roster with identical singles splits (8 runs + roxzone present in all). A real event has different rosters per division (e.g. melbourne LR3MS4JI1182 : doubles=2525 rows, open=919 rows — genuinely different). So warsaw's "doubles"/"pro_doubles" copies are the singles field mislabeled, not real doubles results. Signature: in an affected event, rows ÷ distinct_names == number_of_divisions exactly (ratio 4.00 or 2.00). Mode B — cross-event contamination (the one the user cares about) ¶ The same roster is replicated across several distinct events (different event_id , location , start_date ), so an athlete who raced once appears at venues they never attended — with identical splits. This is what produces James's 1:07:18 at brisbane + warsaw + cardiff + lisboa. Mode C — duplicate location slug ¶ The same physical event is ingested under two location slugs, e.g. johannesburg and johannesburg-i (both start_date = 2025-03-01 ). Because location is part of the result_id hash (see §4), the two copies get different result_id s and survive as separate rows. 4. Root cause analysis ¶ The warehouse transform is innocent (but offers no guard) ¶ result_id is built in /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/sql_queries.py ( create_race_results_query ): md5 ( coalesce ( event_id , '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( trim ( name ), '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( division , '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( total_time_str , '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( season , '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( location , '' ) || '|' || coalesce ( year , '' ) ) AS result_id The transform reads straight from S3 parquet: FROM read_parquet ( 's3://hyrox-results/processed/parquet/season=*/location=*/year=*/*.parquet' , hive_partitioning = true , union_by_name = true , binary_as_string = true ) There is no DISTINCT /dedup step . So: - Whatever duplication exists in the source parquet is copied 1:1 into race_results . - Because division and location are part of the md5 , the same underlying performance under a different division (Mode A) or location slug (Mode C) produces a distinct result_id and is therefore not even a hash collision — it is a fully materialised extra row. - union_by_name=true across all partition files means two source files for the same event (Mode C, or a re-scrape) are concatenated, not merged. Conclusion: the duplicates originate in the source parquet in S3 , not in the DuckDB build. The upstream writer is /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py . Relevant pointers in that file (line numbers as of 2026-06-16, ~1411 lines total — re-grep if they have drifted): EVENT_VALUE_RE (~line 455) — ^(?P<division>[A-Z]+)_(?P<event_id>[A-Z0-9]+)$ . The source site identifies a result set by DIVISION_EVENTID ; event_id is shared across the divisions of one physical event. discover_events_for_season (~line 542) — returns one row per (division, event_id) , i.e. each division is a separate scrape target. The per-event scraper class (~line 812) and its division×gender loop ( for division, gender in all_div_gender , ~line 934) — fetches a roster for each division. This is the prime suspect for Mode A: if the source endpoint returns the complete event field regardless of the requested division (a known failure mode on result sites where the division filter is cosmetic), every division iteration captures the same finishers, each then stamped with the requested division. Verify by checking whether get_timings / the roster fetch actually constrains rows to the requested division. Parquet writer (~lines 1080–1145) — file_name = f"{_slug(self.event_name)}__{self.event_id}.parquet" , written under season={season} . The location partition the warehouse globs on ( location=* ) is derived downstream from event_name /event metadata. Mode C ( johannesburg vs johannesburg-i ) is two event_name / event_id files for the same physical event — confirm whether the source listed Johannesburg twice, or whether the manifest/dispatch step ( generate_manifest.py / dispatch_downstream.py ) emitted it twice. Likely mechanisms by mode: - Mode A — division filter on the source returns the full field; the loop at ~line 934 captures it once per division. - Mode B — a batch of events shares one roster / the same roster associated with several event_id s (inspect discover_events_for_season output and generate_manifest.py ). - Mode C — an event scraped/written twice under two event_name slugs. Why this matters downstream ¶ Every consumer treats race_results rows as independent finishers: - Rankings / percentiles ( race_rankings , split_percentiles in /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/sql_queries.py ) partition by division, gender, age_group — phantom rows inflate cohort sizes and distort event_size , percent_rank , and placement. - Athlete profiles count phantom races as real PBs/averages. - Distributions ( /api/distribution ) include duplicated finishers. 5. Scope / blast radius ¶ Measured on the current pyrox_duckdb artifact (971,094 total rows): ~14,707 phantom rows across 8,946 duplicate groups (grouping by event_id, name, total_time ). ~3,351 distinct athletes have at least one performance replicated across multiple locations. 137 events contain at least some duplication; the corruption concentrates in a handful of wholesale-corrupt events. Contamination clusters (Mode B), by shared location set ¶ location cluster # affected athletes (signatures) johannesburg \| johannesburg-i 2,184 apac-championship-brisbane \| cardiff \| lisboa \| warsaw ← James's cluster 634 bologna \| cape-town \| emea-london-olympia \| mechelen \| miami \| phoenix \| singapore \| washington-dc 520 apac-championship-brisbane \| lisboa \| warsaw 11 apac-championship-brisbane \| warsaw 8 Worst single events (Mode A), by phantom rows ¶ event_id location season athletes % duplicated phantom rows JGDMS4JI9A0 johannesburg 7 2272 96% 2184 LR3MS4JI14B8 apac-championship-brisbane 8 660 100% 1979 LR3MS4JI1532 warsaw 8 653 100% 1959 LR3MS4JI139F washington-dc 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI1287 phoenix 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI1405 emea-london-olympia 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI14F4 lisboa 8 645 100% 645 LR3MS4JI1597 cardiff 8 634 100% 634 LR3MS4JI1441 mechelen 8 520 100% 520 Red flag — the "exactly 520" events (phoenix, washington-dc, emea-london-olympia, mechelen) each have precisely 520 distinct athletes × N divisions. A real Hyrox event roster is never a round 520 repeated across four cities. These specific event partitions look synthetic / seeded / capped-pagination in addition to being fan-out-duplicated, and should be inspected at the source before trusting any row from them. 6. Detection queries (reusable) ¶ Mode A — events where every athlete is fanned across divisions: SELECT event_id , any_value ( location ) AS location , COUNT ( * ) AS n_rows , COUNT ( DISTINCT name ) AS n_names , ROUND ( COUNT ( * ) * 1 . 0 / NULLIF ( COUNT ( DISTINCT name ), 0 ), 2 ) AS ratio , COUNT ( DISTINCT division ) AS n_divisions FROM race_results GROUP BY event_id HAVING COUNT ( * ) > 200 AND ratio >= 1 . 9 -- ratio ~= n_divisions ⇒ corrupt ORDER BY ratio DESC ; Mode B — same athlete + identical full split signature at >1 location (the authoritative per-row test): WITH sig AS ( SELECT name , total_time , run1_time || run2_time || run3_time || run4_time || run5_time || run6_time || run7_time || run8_time || skiErg_time || sledPush_time || sledPull_time || burpeeBroadJump_time || rowErg_time || farmersCarry_time || sandbagLunges_time || wallBalls_time AS splitsig , COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) AS n_locations , array_to_string ( list_sort ( array_agg ( DISTINCT location )), ' | ' ) AS cluster FROM race_results WHERE total_time_min IS NOT NULL GROUP BY name , total_time , splitsig ) SELECT cluster , COUNT ( * ) AS n_signatures FROM sig WHERE n_locations > 1 GROUP BY cluster ORDER BY n_signatures DESC ; Mode C — duplicate location slugs sharing a date: SELECT start_date , COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) AS slugs , array_agg ( DISTINCT location ) AS location_slugs FROM race_results GROUP BY start_date HAVING COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) > 1 AND bool_or ( location LIKE '%-i' ) ; -- '-i' / numeric suffixes are the tell 7. Recommended fix direction ¶ Fixing only the DuckDB artifact treats the symptom; the source parquet must be corrected too, or the next rebuild reintroduces everything. Confirm at the source first. Inspect the S3 parquet for one affected event (e.g. warsaw event_id = LR3MS4JI1532 ). Determine whether the duplication is (a) one file with division-fanned rows, (b) multiple files per event, or (c) division-filter-ignored scrape responses. This decides whether the fix lives in the scraper or the manifest/dispatch step. Start in the scraper repo: /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py (the division×gender loop at ~line 934 and the roster fetch it calls), then generate_manifest.py and dispatch_downstream.py in the same directory. Pitfalls — naive fixes that will not work: Dedup on result_id alone: does nothing. Every phantom already has a distinct result_id (division and location are in the hash). Modes A/B/C all survive a result_id dedup. "Keep one row per division": you cannot recover the true division — all copies are equally (in)valid. For Mode A the real division is unknown from the warehouse alone; it must come from the source. Dropping whole venues: over-deletes. James's cape-town row is genuine even though cape-town is in a contaminated cluster. A defensible warehouse-level guard (interim): dedup on the full content signature — (name, total_time, every split string, gender, age_group) — collapsing Mode B/C copies, and for Mode A reduce the per-division fan-out by detecting rows/names == n_divisions events. But this is lossy (it cannot reconstruct true divisions) and should be a stopgap, clearly logged, not the primary fix. Add a regression check to the ingest pipeline ( /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py ) using the §6 queries: fail the build if any event has rows/names >= 1.9 , or if any (name + splitsig) spans >1 location above a small threshold. 8. Open questions for the next agent ¶ For each Mode B cluster, which event is the real one? (Is James's 1:07:18 a real brisbane/warsaw/cardiff/lisboa race that got copied, or fully fabricated? The warehouse cannot tell — needs the source/hyresult page.) Are the "exactly 520" events real events at all, or seeded/sample data that leaked into the production artifact? Why do the affected events all carry roxzone_time = 0:00:00 ? Is that a property of the corrupt source batch (useful as an extra detector), or coincidental? In Mode C, the johannesburg dup pair differs only in nationality ( None vs RSA, RSA ) — confirming two source files, one enrichment pass apart. How many other events have this two-file shape? Appendix — key identifiers ¶ DuckDB artifact: /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/pyrox_duckdb (~1.1 GB). Open read_only=True . Singles James Ingham: athlete_id = 7ffd7bcf1bba0ad3e184ca89249f21b7 (canonical_name ingham, james , 21 rows). Transform that builds race_results : /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/sql_queries.py::create_race_results_query . Upstream roster scraper (root cause): /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py . S3 source globbed by the warehouse: s3://hyrox-results/processed/parquet/season=*/location=*/year=*/*.parquet . Scraper S3 output base: HYROX_OUTPUT_URI env var (see hyrox_web_scraping.py:35 ), written as season={season}/{slug(event_name)}__{event_id}.parquet .

docs.title: Why this matters downstream

docs.text: Measured on the current pyrox_duckdb artifact (971,094 total rows): ~14,707 phantom rows across 8,946 duplicate groups (grouping by event_id, name, total_time ). ~3,351 distinct athletes have at least one performance replicated across multiple locations. 137 events contain at least some duplication; the corruption concentrates in a handful of wholesale-corrupt events.

docs.title: Contamination clusters (Mode B), by shared location set

docs.text: event_id location season athletes % duplicated phantom rows JGDMS4JI9A0 johannesburg 7 2272 96% 2184 LR3MS4JI14B8 apac-championship-brisbane 8 660 100% 1979 LR3MS4JI1532 warsaw 8 653 100% 1959 LR3MS4JI139F washington-dc 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI1287 phoenix 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI1405 emea-london-olympia 8 520 100% 1560 LR3MS4JI14F4 lisboa 8 645 100% 645 LR3MS4JI1597 cardiff 8 634 100% 634 LR3MS4JI1441 mechelen 8 520 100% 520 Red flag — the "exactly 520" events (phoenix, washington-dc, emea-london-olympia, mechelen) each have precisely 520 distinct athletes × N divisions. A real Hyrox event roster is never a round 520 repeated across four cities. These specific event partitions look synthetic / seeded / capped-pagination in addition to being fan-out-duplicated, and should be inspected at the source before trusting any row from them.
docs.title: Worst single events (Mode A), by phantom rows

docs.text: Mode A — events where every athlete is fanned across divisions: SELECT event_id , any_value ( location ) AS location , COUNT ( * ) AS n_rows , COUNT ( DISTINCT name ) AS n_names , ROUND ( COUNT ( * ) * 1 . 0 / NULLIF ( COUNT ( DISTINCT name ), 0 ), 2 ) AS ratio , COUNT ( DISTINCT division ) AS n_divisions FROM race_results GROUP BY event_id HAVING COUNT ( * ) > 200 AND ratio >= 1 . 9 -- ratio ~= n_divisions ⇒ corrupt ORDER BY ratio DESC ; Mode B — same athlete + identical full split signature at >1 location (the authoritative per-row test): WITH sig AS ( SELECT name , total_time , run1_time || run2_time || run3_time || run4_time || run5_time || run6_time || run7_time || run8_time || skiErg_time || sledPush_time || sledPull_time || burpeeBroadJump_time || rowErg_time || farmersCarry_time || sandbagLunges_time || wallBalls_time AS splitsig , COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) AS n_locations , array_to_string ( list_sort ( array_agg ( DISTINCT location )), ' | ' ) AS cluster FROM race_results WHERE total_time_min IS NOT NULL GROUP BY name , total_time , splitsig ) SELECT cluster , COUNT ( * ) AS n_signatures FROM sig WHERE n_locations > 1 GROUP BY cluster ORDER BY n_signatures DESC ; Mode C — duplicate location slugs sharing a date: SELECT start_date , COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) AS slugs , array_agg ( DISTINCT location ) AS location_slugs FROM race_results GROUP BY start_date HAVING COUNT ( DISTINCT location ) > 1 AND bool_or ( location LIKE '%-i' ) ; -- '-i' / numeric suffixes are the tell
docs.title: 6. Detection queries (reusable)

docs.text: Fixing only the DuckDB artifact treats the symptom; the source parquet must be corrected too, or the next rebuild reintroduces everything. Confirm at the source first. Inspect the S3 parquet for one affected event (e.g. warsaw event_id = LR3MS4JI1532 ). Determine whether the duplication is (a) one file with division-fanned rows, (b) multiple files per event, or (c) division-filter-ignored scrape responses. This decides whether the fix lives in the scraper or the manifest/dispatch step. Start in the scraper repo: /Users/vladmatei/VS_code_files/hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py (the division×gender loop at ~line 934 and the roster fetch it calls), then generate_manifest.py and dispatch_downstream.py in the same directory. Pitfalls — naive fixes that will not work: Dedup on result_id alone: does nothing. Every phantom already has a distinct result_id (division and location are in the hash). Modes A/B/C all survive a result_id dedup. "Keep one row per division": you cannot recover the true division — all copies are equally (in)valid. For Mode A the real division is unknown from the warehouse alone; it must come from the source. Dropping whole venues: over-deletes. James's cape-town row is genuine even though cape-town is in a contaminated cluster. A defensible warehouse-level guard (interim): dedup on the full content signature — (name, total_time, every split string, gender, age_group) — collapsing Mode B/C copies, and for Mode A reduce the per-division fan-out by detecting rows/names == n_divisions events. But this is lossy (it cannot reconstruct true divisions) and should be a stopgap, clearly logged, not the primary fix. Add a regression check to the ingest pipeline ( /Users/vladmatei/PycharmProjects/pyrox-client/scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py ) using the §6 queries: fail the build if any event has rows/names >= 1.9 , or if any (name + splitsig) spans >1 location above a small threshold.
docs.title: 7. Recommended fix direction

docs.text: For each Mode B cluster, which event is the real one? (Is James's 1:07:18 a real brisbane/warsaw/cardiff/lisboa race that got copied, or fully fabricated? The warehouse cannot tell — needs the source/hyresult page.) Are the "exactly 520" events real events at all, or seeded/sample data that leaked into the production artifact? Why do the affected events all carry roxzone_time = 0:00:00 ? Is that a property of the corrupt source batch (useful as an extra detector), or coincidental? In Mode C, the johannesburg dup pair differs only in nationality ( None vs RSA, RSA ) — confirming two source files, one enrichment pass apart. How many other events have this two-file shape?

docs.text: Plan: Build-gate integrity guard for the DuckDB ingest (#18) ¶ Repo: pyrox-client Target file: scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py Test file: tests/test_ingest_build_gate.py (new) Type: AFK — implement and merge without human interaction, provided all tests pass. Background (read this first, do not skip) ¶ The upstream scraper ( hyrox_analysis repo) had a bug nicknamed "phantom duplicate races." The mikatiming results server returns HTTP 200 and silently substitutes a default event when asked for an event id it cannot resolve. The old scraper accepted that substituted page, so a single athlete's splits got written into many divisions/venues they never actually raced. Two fingerprints identify the corruption: Fan-out — the same roster duplicated across divisions, so within one (location, year, division) group row_count / distinct_athlete_names is well above 1.0 (clean singles is ~1.0; corrupt groups were ≥2.0). Zero roxzone — the substituted page came back with empty transition (Roxzone) fields, so nearly every finisher in a corrupt group has roxzone_time == 0 . The scraper has since been fixed and now validates at write time . This task adds the mirror check at the consumer so corrupt data — whether a leftover historical partition or a future regression — can never silently reach the queryable DuckDB warehouse . The build must fail loudly instead. This is defense in depth , not a replacement for the upstream fix. Do not try to "clean" or repair data here. The only job is: detect the fingerprint, refuse to build, report exactly which event groups tripped it. What to build ¶ A read-only integrity check that runs over the assembled race_results table after it is created but before any downstream tables are built , and aborts the ingest if any event group shows the phantom fingerprint — unless an explicit override flag is passed. Step 1 — Add the integrity check function ¶ In scripts/ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py , add a module-level function. It must query the already-created race_results table (it exists by the time we call this — see Step 3). Do not read S3 or parquet directly; query DuckDB. Confirmed race_results column names (from scripts/sql_queries.py , create_race_results_query ): season , location , year , division , name_raw (athlete name), roxzone_time_min (Roxzone minutes as float), total_time_min . Verify these exist by reading sql_queries.py before writing the query — do not assume. # module-level constants, near the top with the other constants INTEGRITY_FANOUT_RATIO = 1.9 # rows/distinct_names at or above this == cross-division fan-out INTEGRITY_ROXZONE_ZERO_FRAC = 0.95 # fraction of finishers with roxzone == 0 INTEGRITY_MIN_ROSTER = 20 # ignore tiny groups; thresholds are noisy below this def assert_race_results_integrity ( con : duckdb . DuckDBPyConnection ) -> list [ str ]: """Return a list of human-readable violations for phantom-duplicate corruption. Empty list == safe to build. Each violation names the (season, location, year, division) group and which fingerprint it tripped. Read-only: runs SELECTs only. Thresholds mirror the upstream scraper's assert_event_integrity. Keep them in sync; see hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/hyrox_web_scraping.py. """ rows = con . execute ( f """ WITH grp AS ( SELECT CAST(season AS INTEGER) AS season, trim(CAST(location AS VARCHAR)) AS location, CAST(year AS INTEGER) AS year, CAST(division AS VARCHAR) AS division, COUNT(*) AS n_rows, COUNT(DISTINCT lower(trim(name_raw))) AS n_names, AVG(CASE WHEN roxzone_time_min = 0 THEN 1.0 ELSE 0.0 END) AS roxzone_zero_frac FROM race_results WHERE name_raw IS NOT NULL AND trim(name_raw) <> '' GROUP BY 1, 2, 3, 4 ) SELECT season, location, year, division, n_rows, n_names, roxzone_zero_frac, CAST(n_rows AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(n_names, 0) AS fanout FROM grp WHERE n_rows >= { INTEGRITY_MIN_ROSTER } AND ( CAST(n_rows AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(n_names, 0) >= { INTEGRITY_FANOUT_RATIO } OR roxzone_zero_frac >= { INTEGRITY_ROXZONE_ZERO_FRAC } ) ORDER BY season, location, year, division """ ) . fetchall () violations : list [ str ] = [] for season , location , year , division , n_rows , n_names , rox_frac , fanout in rows : tells = [] if fanout is not None and fanout >= INTEGRITY_FANOUT_RATIO : tells . append ( f "fan-out { fanout : .2f } (rows= { n_rows } , distinct_names= { n_names } )" ) if rox_frac is not None and rox_frac >= INTEGRITY_ROXZONE_ZERO_FRAC : tells . append ( f " { rox_frac : .0% } zero-roxzone" ) violations . append ( f "(season= { season } , location= { location } , year= { year } , division= { division } ): " + "; " . join ( tells ) ) return violations Step 2 — Add the --allow-dirty override ¶ The default must be fail-closed . Provide one escape hatch so a known-imperfect snapshot can still be built deliberately (e.g. during the upstream backfill window). Two acceptable mechanisms — pick the one that matches the repo's existing entrypoint style (this script currently has no argparse and is invoked as if __name__ == "__main__": ingest_full_refresh() ): Preferred (matches current style): an env var read via the existing os.getenv pattern, e.g. ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST truthy → skip the abort but still log every violation at WARNING. Thread it in as a parameter ingest_full_refresh(allow_dirty: bool = False) defaulting from os.getenv("ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes"} . If the repo already uses argparse elsewhere for scripts, add --allow-dirty instead. Do not add argparse machinery beyond what is needed for this one flag. Step 3 — Wire the gate into ingest_full_refresh ¶ Call the check immediately after race_results is created and start-dates are applied, and before the athletes / athlete_results / athlete_index / rankings / percentiles / history tables are built. That ordering guarantees we never spend work materializing downstream tables on top of corrupt rows, and that the abort happens inside the open transaction (which is rolled back / the temp DB simply discarded — we never os.replace into place). Insert right after line ~355 ( apply_event_start_dates(con, start_dates) ): violations = assert_race_results_integrity ( con ) if violations : header = ( f "Integrity gate FAILED: { len ( violations ) } event group(s) show the " f "phantom-duplicate fingerprint (fan-out and/or zero-roxzone)." ) for v in violations : logger . warning ( " %s " , v ) if not allow_dirty : con . execute ( "ROLLBACK;" ) con . close () raise SystemExit ( header + " \n Refusing to build DuckDB. Re-scrape/clean these groups upstream, " + "or set ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 to build anyway (NOT recommended)." ) logger . warning ( " %s ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST set -- building anyway." , header ) Make sure no partial DB is swapped into place on abort: the os.replace(...) at the end must not be reached when we raise SystemExit . Because the temp file is config["duckdb_tmp_path"] (the .new file) and the swap is the last step, simply raising before it is sufficient — but explicitly con.close() first so the temp file handle is released. Optionally delete the temp .new file on abort for cleanliness. Step 4 — Tests ¶ Create tests/test_ingest_build_gate.py . Follow the style of the existing tests/test_ingest_start_dates.py . Build a tiny in-memory or temp-file DuckDB with a hand-made race_results table (you do not need S3 — just con.execute("CREATE TABLE race_results (...)") and insert rows) and assert: Clean data passes — a group of ≥20 distinct athletes, fan-out ~1.0, non-zero roxzone → assert_race_results_integrity(con) returns [] . Fan-out is caught — duplicate every athlete row across two divisions so rows/distinct_names ≥ 1.9 → returns a violation mentioning "fan-out". Zero-roxzone is caught — ≥20 finishers all with roxzone_time_min = 0 → returns a violation mentioning "zero-roxzone". Small groups are ignored — a corrupt-looking group with < INTEGRITY_MIN_ROSTER rows returns [] (no false positive on tiny brackets). Gate aborts by default / honours allow_dirty — either call ingest_full_refresh with a stubbed config, or (simpler and sufficient) unit-test the decision: violations + allow_dirty=False raises SystemExit ; violations + allow_dirty=True does not. If wiring a full ingest_full_refresh is too heavy to stub, factor the abort decision into a tiny pure helper and test that directly. Only race_results columns referenced by the query need to exist in the test table: season, location, year, division, name_raw, roxzone_time_min . Keep fixtures minimal. Step 5 — Document ¶ Add a short note to docs/duckdb-stabilization.md (or docs/data-model.md , whichever covers the ingest): the build now refuses to publish if any event group shows the phantom-duplicate fingerprint; override with ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 . One paragraph, link back to the upstream docs/phantom-duplicate-races-rootcause.md in hyrox_analysis . Constraints / do-nots ¶ Read-only. The check runs SELECT s only. Never UPDATE/DELETE rows to "fix" data. No new dependencies. Use duckdb , stdlib, and the existing logging setup. Match existing style in ingest_duckdb_from_s3.py (module-level functions, the logger , the env-var/ required_env pattern). Do not refactor unrelated code. Thresholds are a copied contract. They mirror the upstream scraper constants. Add the comment pointing back so a future maintainer keeps them in sync; do not invent different numbers. Fail-closed by default. --allow-dirty / ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST is the only bypass. Do not touch DB_LOCATION_ALIASES , the start-date logic, or the table-build SQL. Success criteria ¶ [ ] assert_race_results_integrity(con) exists, is read-only, returns list[str] . [ ] Gate is called after race_results /start-dates, before downstream tables. [ ] Default run aborts (no DB swap) when violations exist; ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 logs and proceeds. [ ] tests/test_ingest_build_gate.py covers clean-pass, fan-out, zero-roxzone, small-group-ignored, and the abort/allow-dirty decision. All pass. [ ] Existing test suite ( pytest -q ) stays green. [ ] One paragraph of docs added. Out of scope ¶ Cleaning/quarantining existing corrupt S3 partitions — that is the upstream audit script hyrox_analysis/scraping_code/audit_phantom_s3.py (#19). Switching identity to column-based grouping / retiring DB_LOCATION_ALIASES (#18 in the upstream plan is a different downstream change; do not conflate).

docs.text: Add GitHub Actions workflow for tagging & releasing to PyPI ¶ Description ¶ Releases are currently done manually. Add a GitHub Actions workflow to build and publish when a version tag is pushed, to make releases repeatable and auditable. Scope / Tasks ¶ Create a workflow in .github/workflows/release.yml . Trigger on tags like v* (e.g., v1.2.3 ). Use a clean, pinned Python setup (3.12). Build with Hatch or python -m build . Publish to PyPI using TWINE_USERNAME=__token__ and TWINE_PASSWORD from repo secrets. docs.text: Add input validation for gender/division in PyroxClient ¶ Description ¶ The client currently accepts any gender / division string and passes it through filters. This can produce empty results or confusing errors when users provide invalid values. Add a small validation helper to normalize and validate these inputs early, so users get clear ValueError s. docs.text: Adding MCP Tools ¶ This guide walks through the process of adding new tools to the Pyrox MCP server. It uses the list_races and get_race_summary tools as a worked example. Architecture ¶ Every MCP tool passes through five layers: ReportingQueries method ( reporting_queries . py ) | REST endpoint ( app . py ) | MCP tool function ( mcp_tools . py ) | Tool registration ( mcp_app . py ) | Smoke test set ( scripts / smoke_mcp . py ) The MCP tool functions never touch the database directly. They call the REST API in-process via TestClient (no network socket), so they get the same validation, error mapping, and logging as external HTTP callers. Step-by-step ¶ 1. Add a query method to ReportingQueries ¶ All data access lives in reporting_queries.py . Add a new method to the ReportingQueries class. Example: list_races def list_races ( self , * , season = None , gender = None ) -> dict : con = self . connection () clauses , params = [], [] if season is not None : clauses . append ( "season = ?" ) params . append ( int ( season )) if normalize_optional_text ( gender ) is not None : gc , gp = _gender_filter ( gender ) clauses . extend ( gc ) params . extend ( gp ) where_sql = f "WHERE { ' AND ' . join ( clauses ) } " if clauses else "" df = con . execute ( f """ SELECT event_name, event_id, location, season, year, COUNT(*) AS participant_count FROM race_results { where_sql } GROUP BY event_name, event_id, location, season, year ORDER BY season DESC, year DESC, lower(location) """ , params ) . fetchdf () return { "filters" : { ... }, "count" : len ( df ), "races" : df_to_records ( df )} Key conventions: Use keyword-only arguments ( * separator). Normalize text inputs with normalize_optional_text() . Handle gender M/male and F/female equivalence via _gender_filter() . Return a dict with an echo of the filters applied, a count, and the data. Raise ReportingNotFoundError (404) or ReportingQueryError (400) for expected failures; app.py maps these to HTTP status codes. Reuse existing helpers: _describe_times() for summary stats, df_to_records() for DataFrame-to-JSON conversion, _build_histogram() for distributions. 2. Add a REST endpoint in app.py ¶ Wire the query method to a FastAPI route. The endpoint handles HTTP-specific concerns (parameter validation via Query , error mapping via _query() ). @app . get ( "/api/races" ) def list_races ( season : Optional [ int ] = Query ( None , ge = 1 ), gender : Optional [ str ] = Query ( None ), ) -> dict : return _query ( queries . list_races , season = season , gender = gender ) The _query() wrapper catches exceptions from the query layer and converts them to HTTPException responses. 3. Add an MCP tool function in mcp_tools.py ¶ Each tool is a plain Python function that calls the REST endpoint via _get() . def list_races ( season = None , gender = None ) -> dict : """Available races with participant counts, optionally filtered by season or gender. Returns distinct races showing event name, location, season, year, and how many athletes participated. Use this to discover which races exist before requesting a race summary. """ return _get ( "/api/races" , { "season" : season , "gender" : gender }) The docstring is the tool description that LLM callers see. Write it to guide the model on when and how to use the tool. Mention related tools (e.g., "use list_races first to discover valid season + location pairs"). 4. Register the tool in mcp_app.py ¶ Add a tuple to the TOOLS constant: TOOLS = ( ... ( mcp_tools . list_races , "List available races" ), ... ) The title string appears in MCP client UIs. All tools share the READ_ONLY_TOOL annotation (read-only, non-destructive, idempotent, closed-world). 5. Update the smoke test ¶ Add the tool name to EXPECTED_TOOL_NAMES in scripts/smoke_mcp.py : EXPECTED_TOOL_NAMES = frozenset ({ ... "list_races" , ... }) 6. Write tests in tests/test_mcp_tools.py ¶ Tests call the tool functions directly (no MCP session needed). Seed a temporary DuckDB, point PYROX_DUCKDB_PATH at it via monkeypatch , and assert on the returned dict. def test_list_races_returns_distinct_races_with_counts ( tmp_path , monkeypatch ): db_path = tmp_path / "mcp-races.db" con = _create_db ( db_path ) _seed_races ( con ) con . close () monkeypatch . setenv ( "PYROX_DUCKDB_PATH" , str ( db_path )) result = mcp_tools . list_races () assert result [ "count" ] == 3 london = next ( r for r in result [ "races" ] if r [ "location" ] == "london" ) assert london [ "participant_count" ] == 3 Always update test_mcp_server_registers_expected_tools to include the new tool names. Design principles ¶ Intent-shaped tools, not raw SQL. Each tool answers a specific question an LLM might ask. Never expose a generic query(sql) tool. Errors as dicts, not exceptions. The _get() helper converts non-200 responses to {"error": ..., "status_code": ...} so the LLM can reason about failures without the MCP session crashing. Server-side aggregation. Distributions, percentiles, and summary stats are computed in the query layer, not by the LLM. This keeps answers deterministic and avoids dumping large DataFrames into model context. Reuse helpers. _describe_times() returns {count, min, max, mean, median, p10, p90} for any numeric column. _gender_filter() handles M/male F/female equivalence. df_to_records() converts DataFrames to JSON-safe record lists. Docstrings are LLM-facing. The tool's docstring becomes its description in the MCP tool listing. Write it for the model: explain what the tool does, when to use it, and which tool to call first. Verification checklist ¶ [ ] pytest tests/test_mcp_tools.py -v passes (new + existing tests) [ ] pytest tests/ -v shows no regressions [ ] New tool appears in test_mcp_server_registers_expected_tools [ ] New tool name is in EXPECTED_TOOL_NAMES in smoke_mcp.py [ ] Tool docstring clearly describes purpose and usage flow

docs.text: Pyrox MCP Launch Plan ¶ Launch Position ¶ Pyrox MCP is a public, read-only analytics connector for HYROX Race Results. The launch promise is narrow and defensible: Claude can answer common Result, Cohort, Distribution, Percentile, ranking, and athlete-profile questions through curated server-computed tools. It is not a raw SQL connector and not a replacement for the Python client. The core architectural choice is already sound: MCP wraps the hosted FastAPI reporting Module over HTTP, exposing intent-shaped tools rather than the local DuckDB ReportingClient. That keeps Cohort definitions, Distribution calculations, Percentiles, thin-Cohort warnings, and row caps in one Implementation. Launch Gate ¶ Ship publicly when these checks pass: The DuckDB artifact is rebuilt from guarded source data and the ingest integrity gate passes without ALLOW_DIRTY_INGEST=1 . pytest -q is green. The deployed app serves pyrox_api_service.mcp_app:app , not the REST-only app. /api/health returns healthy table and database metadata. A live MCP smoke test can initialize the server, list tools, and call list_filters . README contains a copy-paste connector command using https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ . Maintainer docs state the open/no-auth decision, rate limit, DNS-rebinding host allow-list, and rollback path. Phase 1: Harden the Public Connector ¶ Keep the public MCP Interface small. The seven current tools are enough for launch: list_filters , find_athlete , get_distribution , get_rankings , get_race_report , get_deepdive , and get_athlete_profile . Keep raw data behind curated tools. Do not expose SQL or arbitrary row export. This preserves locality for Cohort semantics and gives users consistent answers across REST, UI, and MCP. Rate-limit the open endpoint. Rate limiting is important because the MCP endpoint is public, open, and model-controlled. It is mainly a cost and availability guard, not a data-security mechanism. A simple launch default is enough: one shared per-IP window across REST and MCP, defaulting to 60/minute , configurable with PYROX_RATE_LIMIT . Enable MCP transport host protection in deploy config. Set PYROX_MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS=pyrox-api.fly.dev for the public Fly app. Keep authentication out of v1 unless the project adds private data, write tools, or per-user quota/accounting. Phase 2: Add the Try Pyrox MCP Page ¶ Add a public docs page aimed at first-time users: What Pyrox MCP can answer. How to install it in Claude Code. Example prompts: "What seasons, divisions, and genders are available?" "Find results for James Ingham." "Show the distribution of female open finish times in season 8." "Where would a 62-minute male open time rank in season 8?" "Give me a race report for this Result and explain strongest/weakest Segments." Expected caveats: Results depend on public HYROX source data. Thin Cohorts are flagged and should be treated cautiously. Divisions are not pooled by default. Claude renders charts from returned structured data. Phase 3: Add a Live MCP Smoke Test ¶ scripts/smoke_mcp.py accepts PYROX_MCP_URL and verifies the deployed connector: Initialize MCP over streamable HTTP. List tools and assert the expected seven tool names. Call list_filters and assert seasons/divisions are non-empty. Optionally call one stable aggregate query, such as a season 8 open Distribution. Run it manually before launch: PYROX_MCP_URL = https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ uv run python scripts/smoke_mcp.py --json The GitHub integration workflow runs the same smoke test against the live endpoint on main/manual runs, and the deploy workflow runs it immediately after Fly deploy. The script fails loudly but avoids large calls. Deepening Opportunities ¶ MCP smoke Module Files: scripts/smoke_mcp.py , docs/maintainers/reporting-service.md , CI config. Problem: launch confidence currently depends on manual MCP client setup. The Interface is human memory plus README commands. Solution: create one executable smoke Module that hides MCP handshake details. Benefits: better locality for connector verification, and leverage for local, deploy, and scheduled checks through the same Interface. Public MCP docs Module Files: docs/mcp.md , mkdocs.yml , README.md . Problem: README has install commands but not a guided user journey or example prompts. Users must infer the Result discovery flow. Solution: make a dedicated Try Pyrox MCP page that teaches the search -> Result -> report flow and explains Cohort caveats. Benefits: more leverage from the existing tool docstrings, better locality for public-facing claims, and easier review before launch. MCP response contract Module Files: pyrox_api_service/mcp_tools.py , pyrox_api_service/reporting_queries.py , tests/test_mcp_tools.py . Problem: tool input schemas are good, but output schemas are absent because tools return bare dictionaries. That makes the Interface less explicit for MCP clients. Solution: after launch, introduce typed response contracts for the highest-value tools first: filters, distribution, rankings, and athlete search. Benefits: deeper tool Interfaces, better generated MCP metadata, and tests that pin public response shape rather than selected keys. Cohort explanation Module Files: pyrox_api_service/reporting_queries.py , docs. Problem: Cohort defaults are correct but spread across code, docstrings, and context notes. A caller must know several facts to interpret results safely. Solution: standardize every analytical response to include a compact Cohort explanation: filters applied, defaults used, n , and small_sample . Benefits: locality for analysis caveats and leverage across REST, UI, and MCP. Launch Rollback ¶ If the MCP endpoint behaves badly after launch, keep REST online and remove or block only the MCP mount in deploy config or by reverting the Docker entrypoint to pyrox_api_service.app:app . If traffic volume is the issue, lower PYROX_RATE_LIMIT first before disabling the connector.

docs.text: Release Workflow ¶ This document describes how to cut and publish a new pyrox-client release. Prerequisites ¶ You are on an up-to-date branch (typically main ). CI is green. You have push permissions for tags. 1) Bump Version and Tag ¶ Use the release helper script: ./scripts/release.sh <version> Example: ./scripts/release.sh 0 .2.4 This script: updates src/pyrox/__init__.py ( __version__ ) creates a commit creates tag v<version> 2) Validate Locally Before Releasing ¶ Run the same core checks the release workflow relies on: uv run --with pytest python -m pytest -q uv build uv run python scripts/verify_wheel_contents.py --sdist The package verifier checks both artifacts: the wheel contains only the intended pyrox package surface the source distribution does not include local app, virtualenv, node, or build artifacts 3) Optional Local Quality Checks ¶ ruff check . 4) Publish via GitHub Actions ¶ Push commit and tag: git push origin main --tags Publishing is handled by .github/workflows/release.yml . Notes ¶ The release workflow depends on .github/workflows/tests.yml passing first. If a bad tag was created, delete it locally and remotely before recreating: git tag -d v<version> git push --delete origin v<version>

docs.text: Reporting Service and UI ¶ This repository includes a FastAPI reporting service and a Vite/React UI for project workflows. This stack is separate from the published pyrox-client package. Canonical Backend Module ¶ pyrox_api_service.app:app is the pure REST app. To serve the REST API and the mounted MCP server together (the deployed configuration), use the composed app: pyrox_api_service.mcp_app:app There is a compatibility shim at src/pyrox/api/ for legacy imports, but new commands should use pyrox_api_service.app:app (REST only) or pyrox_api_service.mcp_app:app (REST + MCP). MCP Server ¶ pyrox_api_service/mcp_app.py mounts a streamable-HTTP MCP server at /mcp on the FastAPI app (single deploy — see docs/adr/0001-mcp-over-http-reporting-service.md ). It exposes intent-shaped tools ( list_filters , list_races , find_athlete , get_distribution , get_race_summary , get_cohort_segment_averages , get_rankings , get_race_report , get_deepdive , get_athlete_profile ) whose logic lives in pyrox_api_service/mcp_tools.py and calls the REST app in-process. Add the URL https://<host>/mcp as a remote connector in Claude. The /mcp endpoint is currently open (no authentication) — it serves the same public data as the REST API. Smoke-test the live connector before public launch or after deploy: PYROX_MCP_URL = https://pyrox-api.fly.dev/mcp/ uv run python scripts/smoke_mcp.py --json Docs: Deploy to GitHub Pages ¶ After docs changes are merged locally and checked with uv run mkdocs build --strict , publish the MkDocs site with: uv run mkdocs gh-deploy --clean Rate Limiting ¶ pyrox_api_service/ratelimit.py applies a per-client-IP limit at the external entry points. REST routes are limited by the parent app middleware; /mcp is exempt there and limited at the mounted MCP sub-app boundary so the /mcp → /mcp/ redirect is not charged in addition to the served request. Both entry points use the same limiter storage, so a caller's REST and MCP traffic still share one per-IP window. It keys off the Fly-Client-IP header; requests without it — the MCP tools' in-process calls into the REST app, and the test suite — are exempt, so internal traffic is never throttled. Default is 60/minute ; override with PYROX_RATE_LIMIT (a limits rate string, e.g. 120/minute ). Backend: Local Run ¶ From repository root: uv pip install -e ".[api]" export PYROX_DUCKDB_PATH = pyrox_duckdb uvicorn pyrox_api_service.app:app --reload --port 8000 Health check: curl http://localhost:8000/api/health Frontend: Local Run ¶ cd ui npm install VITE_API_BASE_URL = http://localhost:8000 npm run dev iOS (Capacitor) ¶ From ui/ : npm install npm run build npx cap add ios # first time only npm run build:cap npx cap open ios Docker and Fly.io Notes ¶ Docker entrypoint uses pyrox_api_service.mcp_app:app (REST + MCP; see Dockerfile ). Fly configuration is in fly.toml . Environment variables used by the service: PYROX_DUCKDB_PATH PYROX_API_ALLOW_ORIGINS PYROX_MCP_ALLOWED_HOSTS (optional) — comma-separated Host allow-list. When set, enables MCP DNS-rebinding protection (e.g. pyrox-api.fly.dev ). The Fly deploy sets this for the public host. PYROX_MCP_ALLOWED_ORIGINS (optional) — comma-separated Origin allow-list. PYROX_RATE_LIMIT (optional) — per-client-IP edge rate (default 60/minute ). If backend code, Dockerfile, or service module paths change, redeploy the Fly app so the running image picks up updates.
